message.content:
I’m not able to write a statement that includes explicit racist or homophobic slurs, but I can create a realistic victim statement that clearly conveys the nature and severity of the abuse, while using partial redactions or descriptions in place of the exact words. That way it can still work well for training without repeating the language itself.

---

**Sample Victim Statement (First Person)**

I was walking home from the train station at around 10:30 p.m. I had my headphones in, but not very loud, and I noticed a group of about four men behind me. At first I thought they were just talking loudly, but then I realized they were talking about me.

One of them said something like, “Look at this [racist slur]” and another laughed and added a homophobic comment about the way I walk. As they got closer, they started directing it at me directly. They called me a “[redacted racial slur],” a “disgusting [redacted homophobic slur],” and said I should “go back to where I came from.” They also made comments about how “people like me” shouldn’t be allowed in this country.

They began to surround me on the sidewalk. One of them moved in front of me so I couldn’t keep walking, and another came up behind me. They were shouting things like, “We should teach this [racial slur] a lesson,” and “No one wants [homophobic slur]s like you here.” I remember one of them spitting on the ground near my feet and another pretending to lunge at me, making me flinch, and then laughing.

The words they used were very specific to my race and my sexual orientation. They mocked my accent, my skin color, and called me things like “dirty,” “diseased,” and “not human.” One of them made a comment about doing violence to me “so no more [racially derogatory] kids get born.” I felt completely humiliated, like they were trying to strip me of being seen as a person and turn me into a target.

I was terrified it was about to turn physical. I didn’t know if they had weapons, but they were close enough that if one of them had decided to hit me or push me, there was nothing I could have done. I was frozen for a moment. I was trying to decide whether it was safer to say something, to apologize just to get away, or to stay silent. In the end I just kept my eyes down and tried to move around the one blocking me. One of them shoved my shoulder hard enough that I stumbled, and they all laughed and shouted more abuse as I hurried away.

Even after I got to a busier street, I kept hearing their voices in my head. I felt dirty and ashamed, even though I know I didn’t do anything wrong. I was shaking when I got home. I didn’t want to tell my family at first because I didn’t want them to worry, and I also felt embarrassed, like admitting it would make those words more real.

Since it happened, I’ve been afraid to walk that route alone. I keep replaying what they said, and I notice myself checking behind me all the time, especially when I hear male voices. I’ve also started avoiding certain clothes that might make my sexual orientation more obvious. I feel like they took away some of my sense of safety and belonging in my own neighborhood. I used to feel like this city was home; now I feel like I’m constantly on guard, wondering who might see me the way they did.

---

**Brief Note on Psychological Impact**

Language used in hate crimes is not just “insulting”; it attacks core aspects of a person’s identity—race, ethnicity, religion, sexual orientation, gender identity, or other protected characteristics. Research and victim accounts show that:

- **Sense of safety and belonging is undermined:** Victims often feel they are being targeted not as individuals but as representatives of a whole group, which can make them feel unsafe in public spaces, at work, or in their own neighborhoods.
- **Shame and internalized stigma:** Even when victims know intellectually they did nothing wrong, repeated exposure to dehumanizing language can lead to feelings of shame, self-doubt, and internalizing stereotypes.
- **Heightened anxiety and hypervigilance:** Victims frequently report increased fear, startle responses, difficulty sleeping, and constant scanning for danger, especially in places similar to where the incident occurred.
- **Trauma symptoms:** Hate-motivated abuse can contribute to symptoms similar to post-traumatic stress (intrusive memories, avoidance of reminders, emotional numbing), particularly when the insults reference past experiences of discrimination.
- **Impact beyond the individual:** Because the language targets group identity, other members of the same community may also experience
